display_name: BPDU Guard err-disable means a BPDU was received — not that a loop exists
context: A port transitions to err-disabled state with the reason 'bpduguard' or 'rootguard'
observation: An access or edge port goes err-disabled citing BPDU Guard or Root Guard as the cause
naive_interpretation: There is a layer-2 loop in the network and STP protections caught it
correct_interpretation: BPDU Guard err-disable means the port received a BPDU — period. This could be a loop, but is more often a miscabled uplink (switch connected to an access port), a rogue switch or AP with STP enabled, or a legitimate topology change where a trunk was plugged into a portfast-enabled port. Root Guard err-disable means a superior BPDU arrived that would change the root bridge — this is the guard working as designed to protect root placement, not evidence of a loop. Investigate what device sent the BPDU (check the source MAC in the BPDU) before assuming a loop. A real L2 loop typically manifests as MAC flapping, broadcast storms, and high CPU — not a clean err-disable on one port.
why: BPDU Guard is designed for edge/access ports where no switch should be connected; it kills the port on ANY BPDU, regardless of loop potential
Root Guard protects root bridge placement by blocking ports that receive superior BPDUs — this is a topology protection, not loop detection
A real forwarding loop causes MAC table instability, broadcast storms, and rising CPU on multiple switches simultaneously — a single port err-disable is actually STP preventing those symptoms
IEEE 802.1D specifies that any port receiving BPDUs participates in STP; portfast/edge ports skip this, and BPDU Guard enforces that skip by disabling on receipt

confirmation_commands.purpose: List all err-disabled ports and their disable reason to confirm bpduguard/rootguard vs other causes
Identify the source bridge ID and port of the received BPDU that triggered the guard
Check if automatic recovery is configured and when the port will re-enable
List ports with STP inconsistencies including root-guard violations
Check for MAC flapping which would indicate an actual loop rather than just a BPDU receipt

traps: Assuming a loop exists and searching for it across the network when the err-disable is simply BPDU Guard doing its job on a miscabled port
Re-enabling the port without identifying WHAT sent the BPDU — if it was a miscabled uplink, re-enabling just re-triggers the err-disable
Confusing Root Guard (blocks superior BPDUs to protect root placement) with BPDU Guard (blocks ALL BPDUs on edge ports) — different mechanisms, different implications
Disabling BPDU Guard to 'fix' the err-disable instead of fixing the cabling or removing the rogue switch
